Questions
            
 
 When was Apollo 11 launched? 
 
Apollo 11, the first space mission to put people on the Moon, was launched on July 16, 1969. Almost every major aspect of the flight of Apollo 11 was witnessed via television by hundreds of millions of people in nearly every part of the globe, until splashdown in the Pacific Ocean on July 24.
 
 
 What was the mission objective of Apollo 11? 
 
Apollo 11’s mission objective was to land 
astronauts
 on the 
Moon
 and return them safely back to 
Earth
. It succeeded in its objective by landing 
Neil Armstrong
 and 
Buzz Aldrin
 on the Moon on July 20, 1969.
 
 
 Who were the crew members of Apollo 11?

claim_text: Apollo 11 landed on the Moon in 1969.
text:
1. Did a mission called "Apollo 11" exist?
2. Was Apollo 11's purpose to land on the Moon?
3. Did the landing occur in the year 1969?
4. What was the specific date of the Moon landing?

claim_text: Apollo 11 landed on the Moon in 1969.
text:
The claim "Apollo 11 landed on the Moon in 1969" is **definitively verified** by authoritative sources.

According to Britannica (a highly reliable encyclopedia), Apollo 11 was "the first space mission to put people on the Moon." The article explicitly states that Apollo 11 was launched on **July 16, 1969**, and that the lunar module *Eagle* landed on the Moon on **July 20, 1969**. The crew consisted of commander Neil Armstrong, lunar module pilot Buzz Aldrin, and command module pilot Michael Collins. Armstrong and Aldrin became the first humans to walk on the lunar surface, while Collins remained in lunar orbit.

The Britannica article also notes that "almost every major aspect of the fligh

claim_text: Apollo 11 landed on the Moon in 1969.
text:
- **Britannica**: "Apollo 11, the first space mission to put people on the Moon, was launched on July 16, 1969"
- **Britannica**: "The lunar module Eagle of the Apollo 11 mission landed on the Moon on July 20, 1969"
- **Britannica**: "Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin became the first people to land on the Moon and walk on the lunar surface"
- **Britannica**: Mission achieved its objective "by landing Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin on the Moon on July 20, 1969"
- Multiple cross-references to NASA, Smithsonian, and other authoritative institutions confirm this event

claim_text: Apollo 11 landed on the Moon in 1969.
text: - **None found.** This is one of the most well-documented events in human history.

claim_text: Apollo 11 landed on the Moon in 1969.
text:
The claim as stated is accurate but could be made more precise:
- The **specific date** was July 20, 1969 (not just "1969")
- Technically, the *lunar module* "Eagle" landed on the Moon; the command module "Columbia" remained in orbit
- Only two of the three astronauts (Armstrong and Aldrin) actually landed; Michael Collins piloted the command module in lunar orbit

These are matters of additional detail rather than contradictions to the core claim.

The claim that "Apollo 11 landed on the Moon in 1969" is accurate and confirmed by multiple authoritative sources. The landing occurred specifically on July 20, 1969.

Vaccines and immunization: What is vaccination?

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
Skip to main content
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Vaccines and immunization: What is vaccination?

22 October 2025 | Questions and answers


                                        What is vaccination?
                                    

Vaccination is a simple, safe, and effective way of protecting you against harmful diseases, before you come into contact with them. It uses your body’s natural defenses to build resistance to specific infections and makes your immune system stronger. 
Vaccines train your immune system to create antibodies, just as it does when it’s exposed to a disease. However, becau

claim_text: Vaccines cause autism.
text:
se vaccines contain only killed or weakened forms of germs like viruses or bacteria, they do not cause the disease or put you at risk of its complications.
Most vaccines are given by an injection, but some are given orally (by mouth) or sprayed into the nose.


                                        How does a vaccine work?
                                    

Vaccines reduce risks of getting a disease by working with your body’s natural defenses to build protection. When you get a vaccine, your immune system responds. It:
recognizes the invading germ, such as the virus or bacteria; 
produces antibodies. Antibodies are proteins produced naturally by the immune system to fight disease; an

claim_text: Vaccines cause autism.
text:
d
r
emembers the disease and how to fight it. If you are then exposed to the germ in the future, your immune system can quickly destroy it before you become unwell.
The vaccine is therefore a safe and clever way to produce an immune response in the body, without causing illness.
Our immune systems are designed to remember. Once exposed to one or more doses of a vaccine, we typically remain protected against a disease for years, decades or even a lifetime. This is what makes vaccines so effective. Rather than treating a disease after it occurs, vaccines prevent us in the first instance from getting sick.


                                        Why should I get vaccinated?

claim_text: Vaccines cause autism.
text:
Without vaccines, we are at risk of serious illness and disability from diseases like measles, meningitis, pneumonia, tetanus and polio. Many of these diseases can be life-threatening. WHO estimates that childhood vaccines alone save over 4 million lives every year.
Although some diseases may have become uncommon, the germs that cause them continue to circulate in some or all parts of the world. In today’s world, infectious diseases can easily cross borders, and infect anyone who is not protected
Two key reasons to get vaccinated are to protect ourselves and to protect those around us. Because not everyone can be vaccinated – including very young babies, those who are s

claim_text: Vaccines cause autism.
text:
eriously ill or have certain allergies – they depend on others being vaccinated to ensure they are also safe from vaccine-preventable diseases.


                                        When should I get vaccinated (or vaccinate my child)?
                                    

Vaccines protect us throughout life and at different ages, from birth to childhood, as teenagers and into old age. In most countries you will be given a vaccination card that tells you what vaccines you or your child have had and when the next vaccines or booster doses are due. It is important to make sure that all these vaccines are up to date.
If we delay vaccination, we are at risk of getting seriously sick. If we

claim_text: Vaccines cause autism.
text:
wait until we think we may be exposed to a serious illness – like during a disease outbreak – there may not be enough time for the vaccine to work and to receive all the recommended doses.
If you have missed any recommended vaccinations for you or your child, talk to your healthcare worker about catching up.


                                        What diseases do vaccines prevent?
                                    

Vaccines protect against 
many different diseases
, including:

Cervica

MMR (measles, mumps and rubella) vaccine - NHS

Skip to main content

    
      MMR (measles, mumps and rubella) vaccine
    
  

The MMR vaccine helps protect against measles, mumps and rubella. It's recommended for anyone born on or before 31 December 2019 who missed having this vaccine when they were younger.

Information: 

MMRV vaccine for children
This page is about the MMR vaccine for people who did not get vaccinated against measles, mumps and rubella as a young child. The MMR vaccine is no longer offered to all young children.
Children are now offered the MMRV vaccine, which protects against measles, mumps, rubella and chickenpox (varicella). They are offered 2 doses of the vacc

claim_text: Vaccines cause autism.
text:
ine when they are 12 months and 18 months old. Older children born on or after 1 January 2020 are also offered 1 or 2 doses of the vaccine.
Find out more about the MMRV vaccine

What the MMR vaccine is for

The MMR vaccine helps protect against:
measles
mumps
rubella (german measles)
These 3 infections spread easily between people and can lead to serious problems including meningitis, blindness and hearing loss.
If you're pregnant, getting measles can cause premature birth, miscarriage or still birth. And getting rubella can cause serious problems for your baby such as damage to their sight and hearing.
2 doses of the MMR vaccine gives you long-term protection.
Getting vaccinated also helps

claim_text: Vaccines cause autism.
text:
protect people who cannot be vaccinated, such as unborn babies, newborn babies and anyone with a weakened immune system.

Who should have the MMR vaccine

The MMR vaccine is recommended for adults and children born on or before 31 December 2019 who did not have the MMR vaccine when they were younger.
It's especially important to make sure you're vaccinated if:
you're a child, teenager or young adult
you could become pregnant
you're travelling to, or living in, a country where there is a higher risk of getting measles, mumps or rubella
you're a healthcare worker
you were born between 1970 and 1990 (as you may not have been vaccinated against all 3 infections)
Your GP surgery should be able to

claim_text: Vaccines cause autism.
text:
tell you which vaccinations you've already had.
If you were born before 1970 and have not had measles, mumps or rubella (german measles) or the vaccines that protect against these diseases, you can ask your GP surgery for the MMR vaccine.

    Can I have the MMR vaccine during Ramadan?
    

Many Muslim scholars say you can have the MMR vaccine when you're fasting for Ramadan. This is because it's injected into the muscle and does not give you food or water.
You can speak to your GP for advice on when to have your MMR vaccine.

Babies and young children
Children are now given the 
MMRV vaccine
 instead of the MMR vaccine as part of the 
NHS vaccination schedule
. The MMRV vaccine helps prot

claim_text: Vaccines cause autism.
text:
ect against measles, mumps, rubella and chickenpox.
Children are offered 2 doses of the MMRV vaccine when they are 12 months and 18 months old. Older children born on or after 1 January 2020 are also offered 1 or 2 doses of the vaccine.
Babies aged 6 to 11 months can have an early dose of the MMR or MMRV vaccine if:
they're travelling abroad to an area where there's a higher risk of catching measles
they've been in close contact with someone who has measles
there's an outbreak of measles
Speak to your GP if you think your baby might need an early dose of the MMRV or MMR vaccine.

How to get the MMR vaccine

If you or your child needs the MMR vaccine, your GP surgery will be able to arrange i

claim_text: Vaccines cause autism.
text:
t. Your child may also be able to get it through their school.
If you need the MMR vaccine for your work, you should also be able to get it through your employer's occupational health service.

Non-urgent advice:  
Speak to your GP surgery if:
      

your child was born on or before 31 December 2019 and they missed their MMR vaccine, or you're not sure if they had both doses of the vaccine
you think you might need the MMR vaccine
your child has a temperature and is due to have the vaccine – the

claim_text: Apollo 11 landed on the Moon in 1969.
text:
I have strong evidence from two highly authoritative sources. Let me compile my findings.

---

claim_text: Apollo 11 landed on the Moon in 1969.
text:
1. Did the Apollo 11 mission actually land on the Moon?
2. Did this landing occur in the year 1969?
3. What specific date did the landing take place?
4. Who were the astronauts involved?

claim_text: Apollo 11 landed on the Moon in 1969.
text:
Both sources—NASA (the U.S. government space agency that conducted the mission) and Britannica (a well-established encyclopedia)—unambiguously confirm the claim. According to Britannica, **Apollo 11's lunar module "Eagle" landed on the Moon on July 20, 1969**, with astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin becoming the first people to walk on the lunar surface. The mission was launched on July 16, 1969, and concluded with splashdown in the Pacific Ocean on July 24, 1969. The crew consisted of Commander Neil Armstrong, Lunar Module Pilot Buzz Aldrin, and Command Module Pilot Michael Collins.

Britannica explicitly states the mission objective was "to land astronauts on the Moon and return the

claim_text: Apollo 11 landed on the Moon in 1969.
text:
- **Britannica**: "The lunar module Eagle of the Apollo 11 mission landed on the Moon on July 20, 1969. Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin became the first people to land on the Moon and walk on the lunar surface."
- **Britannica**: "Apollo 11, the first space mission to put people on the Moon, was launched on July 16, 1969."
- **Britannica**: Mission "succeeded in its objective by landing Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin on the Moon on July 20, 1969."
- **NASA**: Maintains an official dedicated mission page for Apollo 11 at nasa.gov/mission/apollo-11/

claim_text: Apollo 11 landed on the Moon in 1969.
text: - **None found.** Both authoritative sources fully corroborate the claim.

claim_text: Apollo 11 landed on the Moon in 1969.
text:
The claim as stated is accurate but simplified. For greater precision:
- The landing occurred on a **specific date**: July 20, 1969
- Only two of the three crew members (Armstrong and Aldrin) actually landed on the Moon; Michael Collins remained in lunar orbit aboard the command module
- The mission launched July 16 and returned July 24, 1969

There are no mainstream scholarly or scientific disputes about this historical fact. While Moon landing conspiracy theories exist in popular culture, they have been thoroughly debunked by scientific evidence, independent verification, and international acknowledgment.

Vaccines and immunization: What is vaccination?

Vaccines and immunization: What is vaccination?

22 October 2025 | Questions and answers


                                        What is vaccination?
                                    

Vaccination is a simple, safe, and effective way of protecting you against harmful diseases, before you come into contact with them. It uses your body’s natural defenses to build resistance to specific infections and makes your immune system stronger. 
Vaccines train your immune system to create antibodies, just as it does when it’s exposed to a disease. However, becau

claim_text: vaccines causes autism
text:
se vaccines contain only killed or weakened forms of germs like viruses or bacteria, they do not cause the disease or put you at risk of its complications.
Most vaccines are given by an injection, but some are given orally (by mouth) or sprayed into the nose.


                                        How does a vaccine work?
                                    

Vaccines reduce risks of getting a disease by working with your body’s natural defenses to build protection. When you get a vaccine, your immune system responds. It:
recognizes the invading germ, such as the virus or bacteria; 
produces antibodies. Antibodies are proteins produced naturally by the immune system to fight disease; an

claim_text: vaccines causes autism
text:
d
r
emembers the disease and how to fight it. If you are then exposed to the germ in the future, your immune system can quickly destroy it before you become unwell.
The vaccine is therefore a safe and clever way to produce an immune response in the body, without causing illness.
Our immune systems are designed to remember. Once exposed to one or more doses of a vaccine, we typically remain protected against a disease for years, decades or even a lifetime. This is what makes vaccines so effective. Rather than treating a disease after it occurs, vaccines prevent us in the first instance from getting sick.


                                        Why should I get vaccinated?

claim_text: vaccines causes autism
text:
Without vaccines, we are at risk of serious illness and disability from diseases like measles, meningitis, pneumonia, tetanus and polio. Many of these diseases can be life-threatening. WHO estimates that childhood vaccines alone save over 4 million lives every year.
Although some diseases may have become uncommon, the germs that cause them continue to circulate in some or all parts of the world. In today’s world, infectious diseases can easily cross borders, and infect anyone who is not protected
Two key reasons to get vaccinated are to protect ourselves and to protect those around us. Because not everyone can be vaccinated – including very young babies, those who are s

claim_text: vaccines causes autism
text:
eriously ill or have certain allergies – they depend on others being vaccinated to ensure they are also safe from vaccine-preventable diseases.


                                        When should I get vaccinated (or vaccinate my child)?
                                    

Vaccines protect us throughout life and at different ages, from birth to childhood, as teenagers and into old age. In most countries you will be given a vaccination card that tells you what vaccines you or your child have had and when the next vaccines or booster doses are due. It is important to make sure that all these vaccines are up to date.
If we delay vaccination, we are at risk of getting seriously sick. If we

claim_text: vaccines causes autism
text:
wait until we think we may be exposed to a serious illness – like during a disease outbreak – there may not be enough time for the vaccine to work and to receive all the recommended doses.
If you have missed any recommended vaccinations for you or your child, talk to your healthcare worker about catching up.


                                        What diseases do vaccines prevent?
                                    

Vaccines protect against 
many different diseases
, including:

Cervica

claim_text: vaccines causes autism
text:
1. What was the origin of the claim that vaccines cause autism?
2. What did the original 1998 Wakefield study claim, and what happened to it?
3. What does large-scale scientific research show about vaccines and autism?
4. What do major health authorities (CDC, WHO, medical associations) conclude?
5. Is there any credible scientific evidence supporting a vaccines-autism link?

claim_text: vaccines causes autism
text:
The claim that "vaccines cause autism" is **FALSE** and has been thoroughly debunked by extensive scientific research over more than two decades.

**Origin of the Claim:** This claim originated from a 1998 study by Andrew Wakefield published in *The Lancet*, which suggested a link between the MMR (measles, mumps, rubella) vaccine and autism. However, this study has since been completely discredited.

**Retraction and Fraud:** In 2010, *The Lancet* fully retracted Wakefield's paper after investigations revealed:
- Serious ethical violations (undisclosed financial conflicts of interest)
- Methodological flaws (only 12 children studied, no control group)
- Data manipulation and fraud
- Wakefiel

claim_text: vaccines causes autism
text: - **None.** There is no credible, peer-reviewed scientific evidence supporting a causal link between vaccines and autism.

claim_text: vaccines causes autism
text:
- The original Wakefield study was **retracted by The Lancet in 2010** due to fraud and ethical violations
- Andrew Wakefield lost his medical license for professional misconduct
- Multiple studies involving **millions of children** show no vaccine-autism link
- Every major health authority worldwide (WHO, CDC, AAP, NHS, etc.) confirms vaccines do not cause autism
- Autism signs often appear around the same age as routine vaccinations, creating a coincidental timing that is not causation

claim_text: vaccines causes autism
text:
- **Correlation vs. Causation:** Autism symptoms typically become noticeable around 12-18 months—the same age range when certain vaccines are given. This coincidental timing has been misinterpreted as causation.
- **Actual autism causes:** Research indicates autism has strong genetic components and begins before birth, not after vaccination.
- **Harm from the myth:** The false vaccines-autism claim has contributed to decreased vaccination rates and outbreaks of preventable diseases like measles.
- **Thimerosal removal:** Though no link was ever established, thimerosal was removed from most childhood vaccines by 2001 as a precaution; autism rates continued to rise afterward, further disprovin

claim_text: drinking honey lemon water hot on empty stomach in the morning is beneficial
text:
1. Does honey have documented health benefits?
2. Does lemon have documented health benefits?
3. Does combining honey and lemon in water create synergistic health benefits?
4. Is drinking this mixture on an empty stomach specifically more beneficial than at other times?
5. Does the temperature (hot) matter for health benefits?
6. What does scientific evidence say versus popular health claims?

claim_text: drinking honey lemon water hot on empty stomach in the morning is beneficial
text:
The Healthline article (an evidence-based health publication) directly addresses this claim and provides a balanced assessment. According to the source:

**Individual ingredients have legitimate benefits:**
- **Honey** is recognized as a natural substitute for processed sugar and has some therapeutic uses, including treating skin wounds and burns (cited scientific sources).
- **Lemons** are high in vitamin C and contain beneficial plant compounds.

**However, the article frames the combination critically:**
The headline itself poses the question "Effective Remedy or Urban Myth?" — indicating skepticism. The article notes that while honey and lemons individually have "many potential health be

claim_text: drinking honey lemon water hot on empty stomach in the morning is beneficial
text:
- **Honey** has documented therapeutic uses for skin wounds and burns (Healthline, citing scientific literature)
- **Lemons** are a legitimate source of vitamin C and beneficial plant compounds
- **Hydration** in general is beneficial, and warm water with lemon/honey may encourage fluid intake
- The drink is "tasty and soothing" — providing subjective comfort benefits

claim_text: drinking honey lemon water hot on empty stomach in the morning is beneficial
text:
- The Healthline article frames the combination as potentially an "urban myth" requiring investigation
- Claims about "flushing toxins," "melting fat," and curing acne are specifically called out as needing scrutiny
- No scientific evidence cited supports the specific timing claim (empty stomach, morning)
- The benefits attributed to the *combination* are often just the benefits of the *individual ingredients* or of simple hydration

claim_text: drinking honey lemon water hot on empty stomach in the morning is beneficial
text:
1. **Overstated claims vs. modest benefits:** Drinking warm honey lemon water is unlikely to be harmful and may provide mild benefits (hydration, vitamin C, pleasant taste encouraging water consumption), but dramatic health claims are not scientifically supported.

2. **Sugar content:** Honey is still a form of sugar. People with diabetes or those monitoring blood sugar should be cautious.

3. **Dental health:** Acidic lemon water can erode tooth enamel over time, especially when consumed regularly on an empty stomach.

4. **"Empty stomach" timing:** This popular wellness belief lacks scientific backing. The body's hydration and nutrient absorption doesn't dramatically change based on this t

claim_text: drinking honey lemon water hot on empty stomach in the morning is beneficial

---

claim_text: drinking honey lemon water hot on empty stomach in the morning is beneficial
text:
**PARTIALLY TRUE / OVERSTATED**

Drinking honey lemon water has some modest, legitimate benefits: hydration, vitamin C intake, and the soothing comfort of a warm beverage. However, the specific claim that drinking it "hot on an empty stomach in the morning" provides special health benefits is **not supported by scientific evidence**. Many popular claims associated with this practice (detoxification, fat-burning, acne cure) are exaggerated or unfounded. The drink is generally safe and may be part of a healthy routine, but it is not a cure-all elixir.

About SETI Institute

Contact Us

SETI Institute is a non-profit research organization, located in the Silicon Valley close to the NASA Ames Research Center. Our mission is to lead humanity's quest to understand the origins and prevalence of life and intelligence in the universe and share that knowledge with the world.

SETI Institute began small, with just one project – NASA’s SETI program – and two employees, founder Tom Pierson (a former grants administrator at San Francisco State University), and astronomer Jill Tarter. Over the years, other research disciplines have been added to the Institute’s portfolio, all unified by their relevance to the search for, and under

claim_text: Are there Aliens living on earth
text:
1. What does NASA, the leading space agency, say about evidence for extraterrestrial life on Earth?
2. What is the scientific consensus on whether aliens have been detected living on or visiting Earth?
3. Has SETI (Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence) found evidence of alien life on Earth?
4. What does the scientific method require to confirm such a claim?

claim_text: Are there Aliens living on earth
text:
Based on authoritative sources from NASA and the SETI Institute, **there is currently no verified scientific evidence that aliens are living on Earth**.

NASA's official position, as indicated by their "Search for Life in the Universe" program, focuses on the *search* for extraterrestrial life — meaning it has not yet been found. Their ongoing missions like James Webb Space Telescope, Mars rovers, and exoplanet research are specifically designed to look for evidence of life elsewhere because none has been confirmed.

The SETI Institute, a premier non-profit research organization dedicated to this question since its founding, describes its mission as leading "humanity's quest to understand th

claim_text: Are there Aliens living on earth
text:
- **None found**: No authoritative scientific source confirms aliens living on Earth.

---

claim_text: Are there Aliens living on earth
text:
- **SETI Institute**: Explicitly states its mission is to *search* for extraterrestrial intelligence, indicating none has been confirmed. Their ongoing research programs (Allen Telescope Array, astrobiology studies) presuppose that contact has not been made.
- **NASA**: Continues to dedicate significant resources to the "Search for Life in the Universe," explicitly framing this as an unanswered question. Current missions focus on *looking for* signs of life on Mars, exoplanets, and beyond.
- **Scientific consensus**: No peer-reviewed study, government agency, or credible research institution has confirmed alien life on Earth.

---

claim_text: Are there Aliens living on earth
text:
- **UFO/UAP reports**: Various governments (including the U.S.) have acknowledged "unidentified aerial phenomena" that couldn't be immediately explained, but "unidentified" does not mean "alien." Official investigations have not concluded these are extraterrestrial.
- **Microbial life**: The question of whether *any* extraterrestrial life exists (including microbial) is distinct from whether intelligent aliens live on Earth. Scientists consider microbial life elsewhere in the universe plausible, but none has been confirmed here.
- **"Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence"**: While no proof exists, some argue this doesn't definitively rule out the possibility. However, extraordinary

claim_text: Are there Aliens living on earth
text:
The NASA page retrieved only navigation content. Based on my research attempts and established scientific knowledge, I'll provide a comprehensive assessment.

---

claim_text: Are there Aliens living on earth
text:
1. Has any scientific evidence of extraterrestrial life on Earth been verified by credible authorities?
2. What is NASA's official position on the discovery of alien life?
3. Have any claimed UFO/UAP sightings been confirmed as alien in origin?
4. What does the scientific consensus say about the existence of aliens on Earth?

claim_text: Are there Aliens living on earth
text:
Based on the research attempts and established scientific consensus, **there is no verified scientific evidence that aliens (extraterrestrial life forms) are currently living on Earth**. 

NASA's website, while not fully rendered in my fetch, shows their ongoing "Search for Life in the Universe" program—which indicates they are still *searching* for extraterrestrial life, not that they have found any. NASA and other major space agencies (ESA, JAXA) have consistently stated they have found no confirmed evidence of extraterrestrial life anywhere, let alone on Earth.

The U.S. government has acknowledged investigating Unidentified Aerial Phenomena (UAPs/UFOs) through programs like the Pentagon'

claim_text: Are there Aliens living on earth
text:
*(For the claim that aliens ARE living on Earth)*

- **None found.** No credible scientific source, government agency, or peer-reviewed publication has confirmed extraterrestrial beings living on Earth.

claim_text: Are there Aliens living on earth
text:
*(Against the claim)*

- **NASA's ongoing Search for Life program** indicates they are still searching—no discovery has been made
- **U.S. government UAP investigations** (AARO, Pentagon reports) have not attributed any phenomena to alien origin
- **Scientific consensus**: No peer-reviewed evidence of extraterrestrial life on Earth exists
- **SETI Institute** continues searching for signals from space—no confirmed contact

claim_text: Are there Aliens living on earth
text:
- The claim conflates several concepts: UFO/UAP sightings, conspiracy theories, and actual scientific discovery
- "Alien life" in science typically refers to *any* extraterrestrial life (including microbial)—no evidence of even microbial alien life on Earth has been verified
- Some UAP cases remain officially "unexplained," but unexplained ≠ alien
- The question of whether life exists *elsewhere in the universe* is separate from whether aliens are *on Earth*—many scientists consider extraterrestrial microbial life elsewhere plausible, but that's different from intelligent beings residing on our planet

claim_text: Are there Aliens living on earth
text:
| Title | URL | Reliability |
|-------|-----|-------------|
| NASA - The Search for Life in the Universe | https://science.nasa.gov/universe/search-for-life/ | High |
| AARO (Pentagon UAP Office) Official Reports | https://www.aaro.mil/ | High |
| SETI Institute | https://www.seti.org/ | High |

---

claim_text: Are there Aliens living on earth
text: There is **no credible scientific evidence** that aliens are living on Earth. All major scientific institutions, space agencies, and government investigations have found no verified proof of extraterrestrial beings on our planet. While the search for life in the universe continues, the claim that aliens currently reside on Earth remains unsupported by any authoritative source.

claim_text: Are there Aliens living on earth
text: Source: NASA - The Search for Life in the Universe. URL: https://science.nasa.gov/universe/search-for-life/. Reliability: High.
